era_context: The Middle Bronze Age in the Levant and Egypt; the Middle Kingdom period in Egypt is ending (c. 1850–1700 BCE), with increasing fragmentation and the rise of the Hyksos in the Delta. In Mesopotamia, the Old Babylonian period is in its final phase under Hammurabi's successors; the Hittite Old Kingdom is consolidating in Anatolia. In the Indus Valley Civilization, urban centres are declining. The Shang dynasty is established in China.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesoamerica (no written chronology for this period)

events.title: Reign of Ammisaduqa, last major Old Babylonian king
events.description: Ammisaduqa rules Babylon, continuing the dynasty founded by Hammurabi. His reign is marked by military campaigns to maintain control over Mesopotamian territories and efforts to preserve the Old Babylonian state against rising pressure from external powers. His astronomical diaries and administrative records provide some of the best-dated evidence for this period. The dating of his reign remains contested among scholars, with conventional chronology placing him c. 1646–1626 BCE, but some scholars argue for dates closer to 1806 BCE.

sources.name: Ammisaduqa's Omen Series and administrative tablets
Historiographic consensus on Old Babylonian chronology

events.certainty_note: Ammisaduqa's reign is well-attested in cuneiform sources, but absolute dating of the Old Babylonian period remains one of the most contested issues in ancient Near Eastern chronology. Multiple competing chronologies place his reign anywhere from c. 1850–1600 BCE. The assignment to 1806 BCE is conventional but not universally accepted.

events.title: Hittite consolidation under Hantili I or successors
events.description: The Hittite Old Kingdom is in its consolidation phase. Following the reign of Hantili I (who overthrew the Anittas dynasty), the Hittites are establishing control over central Anatolia and beginning to expand militarily. This period sees the development of Hittite administrative and military structures that will define the empire.
key_figures: Hantili I or his successors (Hittite kings)

sources.name: Hittite royal annals and later compilations (e.g., Telepinu Edict)

events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology for the Old Kingdom is poorly anchored to absolute dates. The assignment to 1806 BCE is conventional; actual dates may vary by ±50 years or more.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Old Babylonian period and Ammisaduqa's reign is highly contested. The 'Middle Chronology' places Ammisaduqa c. 1646–1626 BCE, while the 'Low Chronology' places him c. 1550–1530 BCE, and the 'High Chronology' c. 1750–1730 BCE. No single absolute date is universally accepted. The assignment of events to 1806 BCE is therefore somewhat arbitrary and reflects one conventional framework rather than consensus.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1806 BCE is heavily dependent on cuneiform sources from Mesopotamia and fragmentary Hittite records. Egyptian sources from this period are sparse and often undated. The chronology of the entire Middle Bronze Age remains contested, with competing systems differing by up to 200 years. Mesopotamian sources are biased toward royal and administrative records; the experiences of common people are largely invisible. Hittite history is known primarily through later compilations and royal inscriptions, which may distort or omit events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Asia have no written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Ammisaduqa's reign continues into the early 1800s BCE; his successors will face the final collapse of the Old Babylonian state.
